reviews.review: the only game that makes any sense

Not too long into my first playthrough of [i]Hexcraft: Harlequin Fair[/i] I found myself venturing into a haunting trainyard, the striking view interrupted by the sharpshooting cowgirl trying to gun down a werewolf, an event I decided not to get involved in. Instead, I carefully made my way into an unlocked hotel room where I found assorted photos of deceased president John Fitzgerald Kennedy plastered over the wall, and understandably transfixed by the sight I temporarily forget about the conflict unfolding outside. A bad decision as it turned out, as I am shortly thereafter mauled to death by said werewolf.

"Immersive Sims" are seeing something of a resurgence among indie developers as of late, and none stand out quite as distinct as Hexcraft: Harlequin Fair. If Gloomwood and Peripeteia seek to be the spiritual successors of Thief & Deus Ex, Hexcraft feels like the result of Ultima's legacy taking an entirely different direction, as if ImSims as a genre strove more to be like Pathologic than any kind of player empowerment sandbox.

The central crux of the Hexcraft games is that every unique named npc acts autonomously in the game world. They each have hidden stats & abilities, as well as a variety of personality traits and localised knowledge about the present game state. Using this information they'll act on their goals, leading to all kinds of dynamic situations. Sometimes you'll enter an area to an unfolding gunfight, or enter a zone to find it under control of a faction that weren't present last time you were there: all emergent outcomes of the game's simulation. Far more importantly, sometimes in the pursuit of progression-relevant items you'll find they've already been snagged by another npc, leaving you to do the detective work of ascertaining who-has-what and facilitating a way to get it back.

To do this you will have to explore the world, and take advantage of every boon you can find. Besides the various weapons you can acquire there's various forms of equipment and consumable items scattered about the world or available to craft that will bolster your stats so you can actually survive combat, and reasonably stand a chance in the game's various dungeons. Truly diligent explorers will find various magic skills hidden in the world that have utility ranging from freely unlocking doors to divining which npc has hold of an item you need. Of course at the end of it all the most effective tool you have is the very characters around you. Sometimes the best way to solve a problem is to simply facilitate a conflict and take advantage in the aftermath. If you ever find yourself struggling to kill someone then hey - get someone else to do it for you.

It's this interplay that makes Harlequin Fair such an interesting experience, the world will not wait for you to act but it will respond to your actions, and no two playthroughs will be the same as a result. And importantly this is all presented in a delightfully old-school fashion. At no point will the game proactively tell you what you have to do or even how to do it, you're just left to intuit as you check your home computer for live updates on the situation around the map, interact with the other characters, and slowly wade through the chaos unfolding - piecing together your goals and then acting on them.

Hexcraft is one of those rare games that has an unapologetic vision of what it wants to be, if you like games that don't hold your hand, then I'd be hard pressed to recommend a more compelling game. One of the few sincerely "hidden" gems I've played in a long, long time. And none of this is accounting for the completely free DLC expansion that essentially acts as a sequel campaign.

Oleander Garden's games are interesting because their particular brand of obtuse design rides a fine line between intentionality and facade. I love obtuse games, but there's a level of finesse that needs to be struck for the mechanics to feel engaging and rewarding as you uncover them - if you fumble this, your game risks appearing shallow because those obtuse mechanics don't show themselves and players don't know where to seek them out and experiment with them, so they stick to the safety of what they know (wander around, pick things up, loop through areas to see if anything's changed). To describe Harlequin Fair as "Vampire the Masquerade x KittyHorrorShow x Pathologic" does it a huge disservice because in the end, it's not really like any of those things unless you count the color palette as a direct reference, or the fact that there's plants in it and you can pick them to make potions, idk. Even the immersive inspirations don't chock up because most of them are "guys will shoot other guys if there's shooting happening" and "you can place an explosive and detonate it later." In reality, it's more like if Blood came out a few years later and someone made a mod for it that allows you to talk to some of the monsters and they say poetic things like "is hell hereditary" and then tell you where you might be able to find someone to shoot in order to steal their tarot card.

Oleander's games appear - and claim - to be deeper than the player is capable of knowing at first glance, or second or third, but (in my experience) they also don't take bold enough steps to provoke players into looking further than their initial reactions. This is the sad reality of being an indie artist, especially when making experimental work; the simple truth is that the average player WON'T look deeper unless provoked to. There are layers here, but either too many to understand *why* we should be playing the game in the first place, or too few to back up the promises made in the game's storefront descriptions. What I find in the core of Harlequin Fair is a game where you wander around looking for floating PNGs until you get bored and see what can be accomplished through violence. Turns out that violence IS the answer, but I still don't know what question it's answering - to continue the vague gameplay loop for a nondescript narrative? To test out the immersive systems that just kind of happen without my knowledge? To find items until the game gives me a win screen? I've had the same problem with a few other famous examples already, if the game doesn't "get good until hour 30," and hour 30 is just more of the same, then why am I spending my time on it?

I hate that I've become jaded towards this sort of thing, given my excitement for this game specifically, and my proclivities towards punishing immersive experiences. Blame it on having a full-time job and a crisis of self? Oleander, if you're reading this, I apologize for disliking your piece of art that was tailor-made for me. I'm still keeping my eye on your portfolio and I cannot wait to see how you grow as an artist.
A very interesting experience, one I know I need to come back to another time. Question though, has anyone found The Fool?
Do you think hell is hereditary? Like, do we pass this on to each other?

Weird as heck but really fun! My one big piece of advice to anyone starting out playing- don't be afraid to cause some havoc! I'm sure it's possible to beat the game without getting a lot of people killed by random monsters, but I sure as heck didn't manage it, lol

I am certain this is a fantastic game for a certain kind of player. I am not that kind of player. I just find the trial-and-error gameplay infuriating: try something, die, restart. Go somewhere, die, restart. Open a random door, get eaten by rats, restart. 
If you've got a lot of patience and like mystery, you might find this enjoyable. For me, I can't stand it. 
I do have to mention the FANTASTIC vibes the game creates. If you're a fan of urban exploration, you might want to pick this up just for the atmosphere alone.

One of the best low-budget games on steam as far as I'm concerned. As a fan of Oleander Garden's other games: The Pagan trilogy, and Hexcraft: Eventide Sigil, I can say with certainty that this is exactly what I expected in the best ways. It feels a bit jank, the graphics are muddy and dated, and it is very frustrating dying with very little warning over and over. It's part of the experience, however. The game is one big immersive-sim-esque puzzle about working out how to get anything done. Leave your morals at the door, rob some people, and acquire black metal shirts to fight off the horrors lurking in dungeons hidden behind shopping malls and alleyways. It's a lo-fi game for sure, but the experience is well worth the 13 dollars.

this was amazing.

it took two hours for me to find my first weapon, and 5 for the mechanics to click for me. still, at every moment of my confusion, i was engaged. it may take a couple or even more playthroughs to understand the story, but one is enough to love how fucking bizarre and unique the gameplay is. there's not even a defined gameplay loop, because of how dynamically the NPCs act in the world.

give it a shot :)
cool weird game, slightly incomprehensible but clearly has a lot of vision behind it
Perfectly flawed and wonderfully vague. Go girl, give me less, I love it.

hype game where you get to go fast and kick ppls ass with guns and sum magic if you find it, as long as you don't die like a loser before you get to that point, but you can always try again
if you put Eventide Sigil on steam, i'll buy it

A very confusing one to review. I liked playing it a lot. I was fortunate enough to play the demo and it assured me I would. But when I boil things down, a lot of flaws got in the way of me enjoying it much more.

- I was enchanted by the spellbook--you unlock spells throughout the game and can use it to mess with the environment. By the time I unlocked any spells I was far and beyond needing any of the sandboxy ones like summoning neutral hostiles to attack people. I couldn't conjure a reason to ever use spells to take anything down when I was never punished for using a gun instead.
- No holds barred, the atmosphere is amazing. It's a creepy game but the bottom line is that it's a survival horror more in practice than concept--it's got no jumpscares and still got me pretty good naturally, plus the style is extremely unique in how crisp and muddy everything looks.
- I actually thoroughly enjoyed being aimless without instruction. I always knew there was something to do somewhere, but that was ruined twice at the end of the game. Once when the item I was looking for was in a part of the map I had already searched thrice, every time the place was empty but I'm guessing I progressed enough to finally spawn something there. The other time I actually had to flat out look up the location of the final item I needed to finish the game because I passed by the not-obvious-enough clue a hundred times, and that proved to be its own herculean task--the only true guide to this game turned out to be a speedrun.
- The combat is the worst part of the game handily. The atmosphere is immaculate and there's all these gadgets you can use to your advantage, and yet every encounter boils down to you and your opponent standing still and shooting at each other. They always stand still, I try to dodge but most of the time I get hit anyway because I'm trying to dodge bullets. When I'm not dodging bullets I'm running away to hopefully heal back up. Everything contributes to an eerie, immersive, genuine experience and then the clunky rigid combat pulls me out of it every time.
- I was pretty disappointed to eventually learn that NPCs [spoiler]die forever, mostly because anything they add to the world is extremely fleeting, they inevitably die during your effort to progress. Their place in the world is the most interesting part of the game, and while it's only natural they'd die permanently, they were gone before I truly knew what I had, and I found it tacky that they're usually replaced with nondescript neutral enemies. I was unable to truly enjoy their mechanics, but at the same time, they were able to give me directions to locked map areas early, which on the surface seems like a neat way to progress freely but led to me progressing strangely, like unlocking one of the last spells first.[/spoiler]
- The free DLC was a pretty sweet addition. Despite all my expectations and knowledge, having slight shifts and a remixed area led to a satisfying second go at experiencing the game, even if it was more combat heavy and less ambiance heavy to its detriment.

In the end, despite all my complaints, discovering this game was intoxicating and I realize that all these gripes are more so tied to a first-time experience. I might play this game again immediately, and I will live with these 'flaws' as characteristics of the game. I'm afraid playing it again won't be nearly as good knowing so much more than I started with, but I know for a fact years later I will revisit this game and fall in love again. I enjoyed this more than enough to seek out other work from this dev, who seems to have a bit of a following and a consistent style. But without a doubt, this game will not be for everyone, and I think 2 hours is more than long enough to decide whether you'd want to refund or not. Try this.
Interesting game but I wouldn't recommend it unless you were familiar with what you are buying already.
apocrypha of a city in eternal twilight

When JFK said: "Kill them all, Vivian."

i felt it

Deviously inscrutable, deceptively simple and deliciously compelling. HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair is a game far more than the sum of its parts, featuring a living world that turns with or without you where no two playthroughs are the same. The Harbour Dawn expansion is similarly excellent, mixing things up just enough for a familiar, yet fresh experience.
this game keeps me up at night, what secrets are left to be uncovered? what is going on? I don't know but it's very compelling

This is the very rare Immersive Sim that doesn't follow in the footsteps of the old Looking Glass/Ion Storm games, but builds a design language of it's own. The world is small but deep, and rewards experimentation. Just go in, try to go somewhere new or find something new, and slowly learn how the world works.

Sadly, for everything I love about the game, there is a problem I fail to tolerate:
[list]
[*]It's cool that the game doesn't hold your hand, but also, it completely fails to articulate the main goal.
[*]There is an interesting simulated world here, but the actual gameplay is extremely unpolished.
[*]Maps are dripping with atmosphere, but running back and forth to your apartment for saving quickly becomes a chore.
[*]The game gives you a lot of items to experiment with, but everything is vague and the inventory is insultingly tight.
[/list]

I liked the demo a lot, so I hate to give a thumbs down, but the full game suffers from its problems just way too much. Despite all of this, I think I'm looking forward to check out the developer's future projects (if there will be any), because I can't deny there is something intriguing about their games.
Perfectly simulates the experience of being a transgender witch girl wandering around at 3 in the morning looking for your girlfriend(?), 10/10!

Very fun im-sim like game. The basic systems (stats, spells, equipment, AI, etc.) are relatively simple, but they come together in a very satisfying way, leading to an illusion of complexity (but in a good way). Casually, there's a fair amount of fun in exploring both the zones and the systems, giving you a lot of small 'ah-ha' or 'oh cool' moments. The emergent interactions in how the AI reacts to certain things - and especially how they can be manipulated in certain ways - is really enjoyable to watch and play with, and can lead to some fun discoveries itself.

The story is... well I'll be honest, I can't really follow it. I couldn't tell you what that means for the quality of the story, it just flat out escapes me. The way it reads, it's either by someone who used hallucinogens at least occasionally as inspiration, or someone with an EXTREMELY good grasp on English and poetry, possibly both. The prose is beautiful, but extremely strange and heavily influenced by pagan and occult views - and from a much farther delve into them than I ever got during my time in those circles.

Fortunately, you don't have to wrap yourself up in the story to enjoy the game. Controls are very tight, systems generally are generally fairly straightforward, RNG is constrained to how NPCs roam and not things like combat, hitboxes are mostly accurate (and usually inaccurate to your benefit more often than not). The zones aren't so massive as to be troublesome, but they're a good size and have some bits worth exploring for.

Casually, I got my first ending in a bit over 3 hours - 3.3 on Steam's playtime. Took me a while after to get the second (of two I'm fairly sure), and I haven't even touched the free DLC that's supposed to be a sort of NG+. I did, however, start speedrunning it, and it's been a blast routing and rerouting as I learned new stuff and got more consistent at the route.

Highly recommended to check out if you like im-sims, but definitely prefer to go at it blind if possible. A lot of the fun for a casual player will come from discovery and exploration. Not everyone likes getting killed by triad over and over while trying to go fast like I do. 

Oh and finally, trans right.

In a word, cryptic. The game will explain nothing, write nothing down for you, and not direct you anywhere in particular. And, with how niche this game is, you're not exactly going to have much luck finding a guide. It's a fascinating game, but it can be daunting to play.
Another gem from Oleander Garden

Weird game, it starts so obtuse and confusing, and that feeling mostly remains even when you hit an ending.  But theres always just enough of a thread in sight to try and figure out if you can pull it.  It feels rewarding, its very hard to google for answers, its nearly all trial and error, but its just doable enough to be able to keep feeling like your making progress.  The only part i didnt like was [spoiler] the hidden wall in st clairs[/spoiler], i dont know how i was meant to know it was there, maybe one of the rivals mentioned it in the rumours tab, but if they did i missed it.  But everything else was a wonderful chaotic mess to unravel.

pure gem of a game.
no handholding.
just going with the flow.

I have no idea how to progress, where to obtain a weapon, how to use the alchemy, what the NPC's are talking about, where I am, who I am, or what my goals are. And I had an absolutely f*cking amazing time. I've only played about an hour of this and I barely progressed at all in this LSD fuelled nightmare, and yet I adored it. I will be giving this another go, and it will probably go roughly the same way with a slight chance of progressing further. And I am completely alright with that.

The first thing I did was go to Chinatown and accidentally rob a wizard who proceeded to chase me down while I threw back grenades desperately screaming until he eventually found me and murdered me.

This game is a nightmare.

I beat it, I still don't get it. And it doesn't matter.

Started a war in St. Clair between cops, crusaders, and horrors from [redacted]. In the end a witch and I were the last living things, and I was too terrified to confront her.

A fever dream of an immsim. Takes some time and lots of experimentation in your room to get used to, but what doesn't? It probably isn't for everyone, but you won't know unless you try. I'm looking forward to going back and figuring out where all the things I missed are.

Jideo,
Vames.
deliberately, uncompromisingly cryptic and broken in all the right ways. honestly one of my favorite games.
I don't have the slightest idea what I'm doing or how to start figuring out what I'm doing but its fun and the atmosphere is cool!
I like it. i want more bigger games from the dev.
lol don't walk up to the shopkeeps with your gun out if you wanna buy stuff

I feel like your experiences with this game will heavily depend on how the emergent, non-linear systems shake out for you. I can see a lot of people having a really special time with this and even if not I think you'll find yourself surprised by how much it packs in to such a small space.
Cool game if you like shit like Goblet's Grotto or that PSX game you played in a bad dream you had once.

Great immersive sim, with some world-puzzle elements. All of its characters feel like they interact with each other, are aware of each other. There are a lot of neat ways to navigate the world, and potentially some interesting ways to deal with the game's situations.

I will say that some of its features feature slightly less than I would have liked - the consequence system, for example, is pretty insignificant if you go for certain objectives that require killing characters. Characters also don't really interact with your actions as if you're a real player. I wish that if this were the intended concept that more of the game was built around it, though I likely missed mechanics in that area due to my rush for progress. Maybe this was an intended theme!

This is a minor issue in an excellent game with some really great prose, interesting locations and immaculate vibes. Also, this is a severely, severely transgender game in unsubtle ways - for one, the first item I collected from my storage was estradiol. Hell yeah.
Feels like a hands off tour through the dementia ridden dreams of a starving artist with a strange grasp of game mechanics

A great immersive sim filled to the brim with incredible esoteric systems, complimented by a delightfully cryptic world with dozens of different characters.
You can shoot up a mall, or have a zombie do it for you, if an angry character looking to take a relic from someone else doesn't start shooting first. You can brew wild concoctions and fight the chinese triad while dressed as a samurai. You can wrack your brain over where the next obscure puzzle piece fits, or what character has it.
If you love immersive sims and system-driven gameplay, please play Harlequin Fair

Strange and captivating.. I've been thinking about this game a lot since I bought it/first played it. It's really calling me back. Highly recommend. The atmosphere is top notch, and the mystery is extremely enticing!

The most immersive simulationy imsim released in the past decade at least. Beautifully esoteric and esoterically beautiful. There's cute girls in it and one time I summoned a spooky gremlin and it caused a mall to get wiped out.

This is an excellent simulation game which really manages to capture the feeling of exploring a game which although unfamiliar at first has a lot of deep simulation going on under the hood. This makes the game feel like a truly alive world where you are rewarded for experimentation once you familiarize yourself with its systems.

The game is also really gorgeous with a strong aesthetic and atmosphere which helps you get lost within it. Definitely recommend.

Dreams and nightmares exist within the illusion of a city. Step on stage and fulfill your role.

HEXCRAFT: Harlequin Fair is an immersive sim, but not quite the same as a Fallout or other such titles. In many ways, this game shows a future where the design principles of the 90s were never abandoned and continued to their inevitable conclusions. I would recommend this game if you're looking for something completely unique and unsettling. Go in with as little information as possible, and maybe you'll understand too.

A great new entry into my favorite subgenre: Immersive Sims That Hate You. Haunting, unapproachable, and chaotic. Felt like an out of control dream. I hate when great games end up underplayed, but something about a system this cryptic existing on the Internet with minimal discussion or explanation around it feels special. I'm happy at least that mystery can still exist in spaces like this.

Ultimately: This rules, buy it. Queer open world games haven't been this good since Barbie Horse Adventures.

Immersively simulate a nightmare. I instigated a gang war in Chinatown so I could plunder tarot cards from those caught in the crossfire. The victims had recited poetry to me moments before. They knew me by name. 

Oleander Garden’s work is consistently haunting, enigmatic, beautiful, infuriating. It’s genuinely fun to bang my head against its walls in an attempt to uncover its secrets. I loved this game. 

Given how unfairly obscure it is, you’re lucky enough to have stumbled upon this page, so don’t let the opportunity go to waste. Buy it already.

Feels like a real interesting way to approach the mystery genre--having the play attempt to interface with this impenetrable space, seemingly against the will of the game. Harlequin Fair promotes this kind of obsessive digging. I love it.

I don't really know what I'm doing a lot of the time, and do find myself getting frustrated at various things in Harlequin Fair but the atmosphere and world are so good. Exploring the maps and finding new stuff in corners I swear I've been through before is so thrilling, and the amount of content swimming around in here is so interesting. I am so interested in seeing what else I can find while meandering through Old Toronto.
What is this game and how did it get into my house
Bloodlines meets a tarot infused fever dream
